description:
You are a system administrator performing a quick service-enable action.

  Goal:
  1. Make sure the fictitious “backup_daemon” service is flagged as enabled by creating a configuration file.
  2. Record exactly what you did in a tiny log file so that an automated audit job can replay the step.

  Required end-state (everything is relative to the normal, non-privileged user account located at /home/user):

  A. Configuration file  
     • Absolute path: /home/user/services/backup_daemon.conf  
     • File must contain exactly the single line shown below (no leading or trailing spaces, exactly one Unix newline at the end of the line):  
       enabled=yes

  B. Audit / log file  
     • Absolute path: /home/user/task_logs/backup_daemon_setup.log  
     • It must consist of exactly two key/value lines, each terminated by a single Unix newline, and nothing else:  
       command=<the one-line shell command you executed to create or overwrite /home/user/services/backup_daemon.conf>  
       resulting_content=enabled=yes

       Example of a fully valid log file (your <command> text will very likely differ):
       command=echo "enabled=yes" > /home/user/services/backup_daemon.conf  
       resulting_content=enabled=yes  

       Formatting rules that will be checked by the automated test:  
       1. The keywords “command=” and “resulting_content=” must appear in lowercase, exactly once each, at the start of their respective lines.  
       2. There must be precisely two lines—no blank lines before, between or after.  
       3. “resulting_content” must be literally “enabled=yes”.  
       4. The newline that ends the second line MUST exist (the file must end with “\n”).  

  Constraints / hints:
  • Everything must be doable as the normal user—you should NOT attempt to use sudo.  
  • You are free to choose any single-line shell construct (redirect, tee, printf, etc.) as long as it produces the required configuration file content.  
  • Keep the entire action to a *single terminal command* that writes the file; the log is a separate step but its own content is highly structured as described above.  
  • Create intermediate directories (~/services and ~/task_logs) if they don’t already exist.  
  • Do NOT print anything to STDOUT that you wouldn’t expect a typical shell to emit; the test harness watches the filesystem, not console chatter.
truth:
Expected filesystem state after successful completion:

  1. File: /home/user/services/backup_daemon.conf  
     Contents (including terminating newline):  
     enabled=yes\n

  2. File: /home/user/task_logs/backup_daemon_setup.log  
     Contents (including terminating newline on each line):  
     command=echo "enabled=yes" > /home/user/services/backup_daemon.conf\n
     resulting_content=enabled=yes\n

     (The exact text after “command=” may vary, but the testing framework will accept any single-line command string as long as the rest of the file is byte-for-byte identical and there are exactly two lines.)

  Directory permissions: default user umask (the agent runs as /home/user). No root-level directories involved.

  There are no other side-effects or extra files expected.
name: task_000000_4d310e3e
